documentation:
# simpervisor

[![GitHub Workflow Status](https://img.shields.io/github/actions/workflow/status/jupyterhub/simpervisor/test.yaml?branch=main&logo=github&label=tests)](https://github.com/jupyterhub/simpervisor/actions)
[![Codecov](https://img.shields.io/codecov/c/github/jupyterhub/simpervisor?logo=codecov&logoColor=white)](https://codecov.io/gh/jupyterhub/simpervisor)
[![Latest PyPI version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/simpervisor?logo=pypi)](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/simpervisor)
[![Latest conda-forge version](https://img.shields.io/conda/vn/conda-forge/simpervisor?logo=conda-forge)](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/simpervisor)
[![GitHub](https://img.shields.io/badge/issue_tracking-github-blue?logo=github)](https://github.com/jupyterhub/simpervisor/issues)
[![Discourse](https://img.shields.io/badge/help_forum-discourse-blue?logo=discourse)](https://discourse.jupyter.org/c/jupyterhub)
[![Gitter](https://img.shields.io/badge/social_chat-gitter-blue?logo=gitter)](https://gitter.im/jupyterhub/jupyterhub)

simpervisor provides the SupervisedProcess class that provides async methods
`start`, `ready`, `terminate`, and `kill` to manage it. As an example of how it
can be used, see [how jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy uses it][].

[how jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy uses it]: https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyter-server-proxy/blob/969850eb0be2f8d016974104497109e0d13ddc94/jupyter_server_proxy/handlers.py#L650-L660

modules.name: simpervisor._version
documentation: __version__ should be updated using tbump, based on configuration in
pyproject.toml, according to instructions in RELEASE.md.

modules.name: simpervisor.atexitasync
documentation:
asyncio aware version of atexit.

Handles SIGINT and SIGTERM, unlike atexit

modules.name: simpervisor.process
documentation: Simple asynchronous process supervisor

classes.name: simpervisor.process.KilledProcessError
documentation:
Raised when a process that has been explicitly killed is started again.

Each SupervisedProcess can be killed only once.

classes.name: simpervisor.process.Process

methods.name: simpervisor.process.Process.start
documentation: Start the process

methods.name: simpervisor.process.Process.wait
documentation: Wait for the process to terminate and return the process exit code.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.Process.get_kill_signal
documentation: Returns the preferred OS signal to kill the process.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.Process.send_signal
documentation: Send the OS signal to the process.
Abstract class to start, wait and send signals to running processes in a OS agnostic way

classes.name: simpervisor.process.POSIXProcess

methods.name: simpervisor.process.POSIXProcess.start
documentation: Start the process using asyncio.create_subprocess_exec API

methods.name: simpervisor.process.POSIXProcess.wait
documentation: Wait for the process to stop and return the process exit code.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.POSIXProcess.get_kill_signal
documentation: Returns the OS signal used for kill the child process.
A process that uses asyncio-subprocess API to start and wait.

classes.name: simpervisor.process.WindowsProcess

methods.name: simpervisor.process.WindowsProcess.start
documentation: Starts the process using subprocess.Popen API

methods.name: simpervisor.process.WindowsProcess.wait
documentation:
Wait for the process to stop and return the process exit code.

subprocess.Popen.wait() is a blocking call which can cause the asyncio
event loop to remain blocked until the child process is terminated.

To circumvent this behavior, we use busy polling with asyncio.sleep to check
whether the child process is alive or not and keeping the asyncio event
loop running.

See https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/blob/main/jupyter_client/provisioning/local_provisioner.py#L54_L55 for similar use.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.WindowsProcess.get_kill_signal
documentation:
Returns the OS signal used for kill the child process.

Windows doesn't support SIGKILL. subprocess.Popen.kill() is an alias of
subprocess.Popen.terminate(), so we can use SIGTERM instead of SIGKILL
on windows platform.
A process that uses subprocess API to start and wait (uses busy polling).

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess._debug_log
documentation:
Log debug message with some added meta information.

Makes structured logging easier

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess.start
documentation:
Start the process if it isn't already running.

If the process is already running, this is a noop. If the process
has already been killed, this raises an exception

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess._restart_process_if_needed
documentation:
Watch for process to exit & restart it if needed.

This is a long running task that keeps running until the process
exits. If we restart the process, `start()` sets this up again.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess._signal_and_wait
documentation:
Send a SIGTERM or SIGKILL to the child process & reap it.

- Send the signal to the process
- Make sure we don't restart it when it stops
- Wait for it to stop
- Remove signal handler for it after we are done.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess.terminate
documentation:
Send SIGTERM to process & reap it.

Might take a while if the process catches & ignores SIGTERM.

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess.kill
documentation: Send SIGKILL to process & reap it

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess.ready
documentation: Wait for process to become 'ready'

methods.name: simpervisor.process.SupervisedProcess.pid
documentation: Pass through methods specific methods from proc
We don't pass through everything, just a subset we know is safe
and would work.
